subsystem: Extension-plugin registry lookup for libyang, the YANG data-modeling library. The function ext_get_plugin() searches a global table of registered YANG extension plugins (ext_plugins / ext_plugins_count) for an entry matching a (name, module, revision) triple and returns the associated plugin descriptor pointer, or NULL when no entry matches. It is the resolver that binds an extension instance parsed from a YANG/YIN schema to the C plugin that implements that extension's semantics.
provenance: Identified by naming and structure rather than an SPDX header (none is present in the shown slice). The symbol names ext_get_plugin, the module-scoped globals ext_plugins and ext_plugins_count, and the (name, module, revision) match key with a per-entry .plugin field are the signature of libyang's extension-plugin subsystem (src/plugins.c, struct lyext_plugin_list with fields name/module/revision/plugin). The sample tag comment on line 1 ("sample: primevul_c_validation_0322_fixed") and the uint16_t loop counter matching libyang's uint16_t ext_plugins_count corroborate this.
trust_boundary: libyang parses YANG/YIN schema modules that are frequently attacker-influenced (NETCONF/RESTCONF servers load modules advertised or supplied by peers, and tooling loads third-party modules). The name and module strings passed here are the extension name and defining-module name lifted out of a parsed extension statement; revision is the module revision string. All three therefore originate on the untrusted side of the schema parser and can be absent (NULL) or arbitrary. The ext_plugins[] table entries are internally registered, but their .name/.module/.revision fields are attacker-comparable and, for revision, may legitimately be NULL. The hostile inputs are the three char* arguments, chiefly whether each is NULL.

what_the_code_does.explanation: Line 1 is a harness annotation comment, not source. Line 2 is the function signature: ext_get_plugin takes three const char* parameters — name (the extension's name), module (the name of the module that defines the extension), and revision (the module's revision date, or NULL for a revision-agnostic match). The return type is not visible in the shown slice (it lives on the line above line 2 that was truncated); from context it returns a plugin descriptor pointer (struct lyext_plugin*). The function's job is to translate a schema-level (name, module, revision) identity into the registered C plugin that handles it.

A single 16-bit unsigned loop index u is declared. Its width is deliberately matched to libyang's ext_plugins_count global, which is itself a uint16_t; the registry can hold at most 65535 plugins. u will iterate the table below.

Two assertions declare a precondition: the caller must pass non-NULL name and module. Note that revision is intentionally NOT asserted here — the code path below treats a NULL revision as a meaningful "match any/none revision" value, so revision is allowed to be NULL by contract while name and module are not. assert() is the only guard standing between a NULL name/module and the strcmp() calls that follow.

A linear scan over the global registry: u runs from 0 up to (but not including) ext_plugins_count. ext_plugins_count is the module-scoped count of currently registered extension plugins. Because both u and ext_plugins_count are uint16_t, the loop terminates cleanly for any count in [0, 65535]. Each iteration inspects ext_plugins[u].

The match predicate. First, the extension name must equal the entry's .name (strcmp returns 0, so !strcmp is true). Second, the module name must equal .module. Third, the revision must match under a two-branch rule: either BOTH the query revision and the stored .revision are NULL (a revision-agnostic entry matched by a revision-agnostic query), OR the query revision is non-NULL AND it string-equals the stored .revision. The && short-circuits, so the revision test is only reached once name and module have already matched exactly. Note the asymmetry baked into the revision branches: the first branch guards the query being NULL, and the second branch guards the query being non-NULL, but the second branch feeds ext_plugins[u].revision straight into strcmp without first checking that the stored revision is non-NULL.

On the first fully-matching entry, the function returns that entry's .plugin descriptor pointer. This is the success path — the caller receives a live plugin pointer it will presumably dereference to invoke the extension's callbacks.

If the scan completes with no match, the function returns NULL. This is the documented "not found" sentinel and is a completely normal, expected outcome (an extension whose plugin was never registered). The entire safety of the caller therefore hinges on the caller treating NULL as "no plugin" and not dereferencing it.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: assert() is used as the sole input validation for name and module, but standard assert() expands to nothing when the translation unit is compiled with -DNDEBUG, which is the norm for release builds. In such a build a NULL name or NULL module is not rejected: control falls straight through to line 10 where strcmp(name, ...) / strcmp(module, ...) dereference the NULL, crashing the process (NULL-pointer dereference, denial of service). Because name/module are schema-derived and can plausibly be NULL when an extension statement is malformed or partially parsed, the invariant "name and module are non-NULL before strcmp" must be enforced with a real runtime check (if (!name || !module) return NULL;), not an assertion. Using assertions for validating untrusted input is the classic reachable-assertion / disabled-check pattern.

The revision comparison is asymmetric about which side may be NULL. The second OR-branch is entered when the caller's revision is non-NULL, and it then calls strcmp(revision, ext_plugins[u].revision) WITHOUT verifying that the stored ext_plugins[u].revision is non-NULL. A registered plugin is permitted to have a NULL .revision (that is precisely what the first branch's !ext_plugins[u].revision test acknowledges). So if the table contains an entry whose .name and .module match the query but whose .revision is NULL, and the caller supplied a non-NULL revision, strcmp() receives NULL as its second argument and dereferences it — a NULL-pointer crash. The invariant the code must uphold is: never pass a possibly-NULL stored revision to strcmp; the branch should read (revision && ext_plugins[u].revision && !strcmp(revision, ext_plugins[u].revision)).

This function has two return values with completely different meanings — a live plugin pointer or NULL for "not registered" — and NULL is a routine, attacker-reachable outcome (any extension referenced by a schema whose plugin was never registered yields NULL). The latent hazard lives in the callers (file not provided): a caller that uses the returned pointer without checking it against NULL will dereference NULL and crash. This is the CWE-252 unchecked-return contract that the file name flags: the safety of the whole subsystem depends on every ext_get_plugin() call site testing the result before use. The function itself is correct here; the risk is the contract it imposes on callers.

strcmp also assumes ext_plugins[u].name and ext_plugins[u].module are non-NULL for every registered entry. name and module are internal registry data rather than direct attacker input, so this is lower severity, but if any registration path ever stores an entry with a NULL .name or .module (e.g. a partially initialized static plugin list), the very first scan against it dereferences NULL. The registration path must guarantee .name and .module are always non-NULL, since this lookup performs no per-entry NULL guard on them.

The loop is safe only because the index u (uint16_t) is exactly as wide as ext_plugins_count (uint16_t in libyang). This is a fragile coupling worth confirming: if ext_plugins_count were ever widened to uint32_t/size_t while u stayed uint16_t, a registry with more than 65535 entries would make u wrap from 65535 back to 0 and the condition u < ext_plugins_count would never become false — an infinite loop / hang. It is not a live bug in the shown code but is a maintenance-time latent defect that the implementation must not introduce.

summary: A small, mostly-correct extension-plugin lookup whose real risks are all NULL-pointer dereferences: name/module are validated only by assert() (compiled out under NDEBUG) before being passed to strcmp; the revision match branch feeds a possibly-NULL stored .revision to strcmp; and, as the file name flags, the function returns NULL for the common not-found case, so any caller that fails to check the result will crash. No memory-corruption or overflow surface is present. Overall risk is medium and centers on denial-of-service via crafted or malformed schema input.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: ext_get_plugin(const char *name, const char *module, const char *revision) — signature (line 2)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Return value is NULL on not-found (line 18); imposes an unchecked-return contract on callers (CWE-252).
Loop index width is coupled to ext_plugins_count's width; latent infinite-loop hazard if the count type is ever widened.
assert used as input validation is removed under NDEBUG, leaving a NULL name reaching strcmp — NULL deref.
Same as name: assert disabled under NDEBUG lets a NULL module reach strcmp.
Bound itself is safe; the only concern is its type relative to u, captured under the uint16_t u anchor.
Dereferences the caller's name (NULL under NDEBUG) and assumes the stored .name is non-NULL.
Same NULL-deref surface for module and the stored .module field.
Index u is strictly bounded by u < ext_plugins_count; no out-of-bounds access.
Asymmetric NULL handling: the non-NULL-query branch passes a possibly-NULL stored .revision to strcmp — NULL deref.
Returns a registered descriptor pointer on a confirmed full match; no defect at the return site itself.
Value returned to caller unchanged; its own validity is the registration path's contract, not this lookup's.
Routine not-found sentinel; source of the CWE-252 unchecked-return contract on callers (folded into the signature finding).

findings.title: assert() used to validate untrusted name/module is compiled out under NDEBUG, allowing NULL-pointer dereference
findings.bug_class: NULL-pointer dereference via disabled input validation (reachable assertion)
findings.invariant: name and module must be proven non-NULL by a runtime check that survives release builds before either is passed to strcmp() on line 10.
findings.failure_mode: In any -DNDEBUG (release) build assert() expands to a no-op, so a NULL name or module supplied from a malformed/partially-parsed extension statement is not rejected and flows into strcmp(name, ...)/strcmp(module, ...) on line 10, dereferencing NULL and crashing the process (denial of service).
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether the production build defines NDEBUG (so assert is stripped) and whether any caller can reach ext_get_plugin with a NULL name/module; replace the asserts with an explicit if (!name || !module) return NULL; guard.

findings.title: Asymmetric revision NULL handling passes NULL to strcmp when a matching entry has a NULL revision
findings.invariant: ext_plugins[u].revision must be confirmed non-NULL before it is passed as the second argument to strcmp; the branch should be (revision && ext_plugins[u].revision && !strcmp(revision, ext_plugins[u].revision)).
findings.failure_mode: When the query revision is non-NULL and a table entry's .name and .module match but its .revision is NULL (a legitimate revision-agnostic registration, acknowledged by the first branch's !ext_plugins[u].revision test), the second branch calls strcmp(revision, NULL), dereferencing NULL and crashing. An attacker who can cause a schema to request a specific revision of an extension whose plugin was registered without a revision triggers it.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether any registered ext_plugins[] entry can have .revision == NULL while sharing a name/module with an entry a caller queries using a non-NULL revision; add the missing ext_plugins[u].revision non-NULL guard.

findings.title: NULL not-found return imposes an unchecked-return contract on callers (CWE-252)
findings.bug_class: Unchecked return value leading to NULL dereference at call sites
findings.invariant: Every ext_get_plugin() call site must test the result against NULL before dereferencing it or invoking any of the plugin's callbacks.
findings.failure_mode: NULL is a routine, attacker-reachable outcome — any extension referenced by a schema whose plugin was never registered returns NULL. A caller that assumes success and immediately uses the returned struct lyext_plugin* dereferences NULL and crashes. The defect is latent in the callers rather than in this function, but the two-valued return contract is what creates it.
findings.what_to_confirm: Audit every ext_get_plugin() call site for a NULL check on the returned pointer before use.

findings.title: strcmp assumes every registered entry's name/module is non-NULL
findings.bug_class: NULL-pointer dereference on stored table fields
findings.invariant: The plugin registration path must guarantee that every ext_plugins[] entry has non-NULL .name and .module, because this lookup performs no per-entry NULL guard on them.
findings.failure_mode: If any registration stores an entry with a NULL .name or .module (e.g. a partially initialized static plugin list), the first scan reaching that entry calls strcmp against NULL and crashes. Lower severity because these fields are internal registry data rather than direct attacker input.
findings.what_to_confirm: Inspect every code path that populates ext_plugins[] to confirm .name and .module are always non-NULL at registration time.

findings.title: Loop index width coupled to ext_plugins_count — latent infinite loop if the count type is widened
findings.anchor: uint16_t u; / for (u = 0; u < ext_plugins_count; u++)
findings.invariant: The loop index u must be at least as wide as the type of ext_plugins_count so that u < ext_plugins_count can eventually become false.
findings.failure_mode: As written the code is safe because ext_plugins_count is uint16_t (max 65535) and u is uint16_t. But if the count were ever widened (uint32_t/size_t) while u stayed 16-bit, a registry of more than 65535 entries would make u wrap 65535 -> 0 and the loop condition would never fail, hanging the process. This is a maintenance-time latent hazard, not a live bug.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the declared type of ext_plugins_count is uint16_t; if it is (or becomes) wider, widen u to match.

audit_checklist: Confirm whether release builds define NDEBUG; if so, replace assert(name)/assert(module) with an explicit if (!name || !module) return NULL; runtime guard.
Add the missing ext_plugins[u].revision non-NULL check to the revision match branch on line 11 before calling strcmp.
Audit every ext_get_plugin() call site to ensure the returned pointer is checked against NULL before dereference (the CWE-252 contract).
Verify that the plugin registration path guarantees non-NULL .name and .module for every ext_plugins[] entry.
Confirm ext_plugins_count is uint16_t so it cannot exceed the range of the uint16_t loop index u; keep the two types in sync.
Determine the true origin of name/module/revision (parsed schema fields) to gauge how directly an attacker can drive NULL or crafted values into this lookup.
